{"step_by_step_thinking": "Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are defined as multipotent cells based on their ability to differentiate into multiple cell types. Multiple documents confirm this property. For instance, Document [15] explicitly states: 'Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are multipotent in that they can form a variety of progenitor cells and then differentiate into cells belonging to the bone, cartilage, muscle, and fat cell lineages.' This directly supports the multipotent nature of MSCs. Additionally, Document [3] mentions that 'MSCs are multipotent and can be harvested from several tissues, eg, bone marrow, adipocytes, and amniotic tissue.' Document [4] and [5] further support their use in bone and cartilage regeneration, which aligns with differentiation into osteoblasts and chondroblasts. The International Society for Cellular Therapy criteria (Document [0]) also include the ability to differentiate into osteoblasts, chondroblasts, and adipocytes, which is a hallmark of multipotency. Therefore, all available evidence confirms that MSCs are multipotent.", "answer_choice": "A"}
